MOTION TO CONTINUE JURY TRIAL
Off Calendar Jan-21-2015 Text Ruling

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alt Summ Adj
Off Calendar Jan-16-2015 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES:Atty. JOHN R. HILLSMAN of McGuinn, Hillsman & Palefsky, Tel No. (415) 421-9292 appearing for Plaintiff.
Attys. PETER A. LINDH and MARKER E. LOVELL, JR. of Gibson, Robb & Lindh, LLP, Tel No. (415) 348-6000 appearing for Defendant.

RULING - A R G U E D - The Court adopted its tentative ruling. DEFENDANT JAMES BRADFORD'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT is denied. DEFENDANT JAMES BRADFORD'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION is granted and denied in part. The Court denies summary adjudication as to issues 1 and 4. Defendant fails to show that he did not owe a duty of care to plaintiffs. Admiralty law does not recognize the assumption of the risk doctrine. See Barber v. Sailing, Inc. (1995) 36 Cal.App.4th 558. Defendant's argument that he owed no duty of care to plaintiffs based on the nature of the activity engaged in is a primary assumption of the risk argument. The Court grants summary adjudication as to issues 2, 3, 5, 6, and 7 [seaman status]. It does not appear that defendant is challenging the first element of Chandris, Inc. v. Latsis (1995) 515 U.S. 347, 368-the requirement that an employee "contribute to the function of the vessel or to the accomplishment of its mission." Plaintiffs', however, cannot establish the second element-the requirement of a substantial connection to a vessel in both duration and nature. Plaintiffs' connection to the boat was not substantial because it was recreational in nature. Plaintiffs were not compensated and had regular land-based employment. With regard to duration, Plaintiffs' connection to the boat was not substantial and the temporal connection was inadequate under the 30 percent rule of thumb cited with approval in Chandris, which denies seaman status to "[a] worker who spends less than about 30 percent of his time in the service of a vessel in navigation." 515 U.S. at 371. The instant case is similar to Xanadu Maritime Trust v. Meyer, 21 F.Supp.2d 1104, 1107 (N.D.Cal.1998). There, the court found that the plaintiff's connection to the boat was not substantial in duration or nature, and that he was not a seaman. The plaintiff was engaged in recreational sailing on defendant's boat and was injured while performing a task related to the sail. The plaintiff was one of several people on whom the defendant called to crew the boat, but had no standing arrangement or commitment to crew. The plaintiff had been out on the boat not more than six times in a span of two years. Xanadu explains that the purpose of the Chandris test is to extend seaman status only to those who in their work are regularly exposed to the perils of the sea. The plaintiffs in this case do not meet that test. The Court denies summary adjudication as to issues 8-10 [vicarious liability]. There is a triable issue of material fact regarding Mr. Cahill's employment-related connection to the vessel and the degree of control that defendant exercised over Mr. Cahill. The evidence demonstrates that defendant owned the vessel, paid Mr. Cahill to serve as the helmsman of the vessel, and defendant was at least partially in charge of the vessel. ORDER SIGNED IN OPEN COURT. Judge: A. James Robertson, II; Clerk: Sean Kane; Reporter: Melanie Dawn Gheno, CSR #7489 (302/AJR)
